description.en: Complete guide to machine screws (cap screws): head types, thread specifications, materials, and applications in electrical panels, machinery, and equipment manufacturing across Africa.
description.zh: 机用螺钉完整指南：头型、螺纹规格、材料及非洲电气面板、机械设备制造应用。
description.es: Guía completa de tornillos para máquina.
description.pt: Guia completo de parafusos de máquina.
description.ja: 皿小ねじの完全ガイド。

keywords: machine screws, cap screws, Phillips head, flat head screws, equipment screws, electrical panel screws, manufacturing fasteners

body.en: Machine screws are externally threaded fasteners designed to mate with a pre-machined female thread (tapped hole) in machinery, equipment, or structural components. Unlike self-tapping screws that create their own thread in soft materials, machine screws require a corresponding nut or threaded hole. They are the standard fasteners for assembling manufactured equipment — from electrical panels to motor housings to control cabinets. Machine screws range from M1.6 to M10 diameter and from 2mm to 200mm length, with standardized head types and drive styles.

Machine screws come in numerous head configurations optimized for different assembly requirements: Pan head — provides a large bearing surface and good drive strength, most common for general equipment assembly. Flat head (countersunk) — sits flush with the material surface for a clean appearance, used in enclosures and covers. Round head — traditional appearance, used in older equipment and decorative applications. Binding head — has an enlarged head with a washer-like bearing surface, used in electrical and battery connections. Fillister head — has a deeper head with a small bearing surface, used where clearance is limited.

Machine screws use standardized thread forms. Metric machine screws use coarse thread (M×pitch) following ISO 261 and 965 standards: M2×0.4, M2.5×0.45, M3×0.5, M4×0.7, M5×0.8, M6×1.0, M8×1.25, M10×1.5 are the most common. Imperial (UNC/UNF) equivalents use Unified threads: #4-40, #6-32, #8-32, #10-32, #10-24, 1/4-20, 1/4-28, 5/16-18. For African procurement sourced primarily from China, metric sizes dominate. Always verify thread compatibility with the equipment's tapped holes before ordering.

Machine screw material determines corrosion resistance and strength: Zinc-plated steel — most common, moderate corrosion resistance, cost-effective for indoor equipment. Hot-dip galvanized — improved corrosion resistance for outdoor and damp environments. Stainless steel 304 — excellent corrosion resistance for food processing, marine, and chemical environments. Stainless steel 316 — superior chloride resistance for coastal African environments. Nylon — for electrical insulation, food-grade, and lightweight applications. For strength, machine screws use property classes similar to bolts: 4.8 (most common, general use), 8.8 (higher strength), 10.9 (high-strength for mechanical joints).

Machine screws are the workhorse of equipment assembly across Africa: Electrical control panels — M3, M4, M5 zinc-plated screws for terminal blocks, DIN rails, and component mounting; Motor junction boxes — M4/M5 stainless or zinc-plated for weatherproofing; Solar inverter mounting — M6/M8 galvanized machine screws for wall mounting brackets; Water pump assemblies — zinc-plated or stainless for submersible pump housings; Generator sets — M5/M6 for control panel and accessory mounting; Switchgear and distribution boards — M4 nickel-plated for electrical contact surfaces.

q.en: What's the difference between a machine screw and a tapping screw?

a.en: Machine screws require a pre-threaded (tapped) hole or nut; they do not create their own thread. Tapping (self-tapping) screws cut or form a thread in soft materials like wood, plastic, or thin metal. For equipment with pre-tapped holes, use machine screws. For assembling sheet metal parts without pre-threading, use self-tapping screws.

q.en: What does 'thread pitch' mean for machine screws?

a.en: Thread pitch is the distance between adjacent threads, measured in millimeters (metric) or threads per inch (imperial). Coarse pitch (e.g., M6×1.0) is standard for most machine screws. Fine pitch (e.g., M6×0.75) is used where higher tension strength or precise adjustment is needed, such as in automotive and aerospace.

q.en: Can machine screws be used for structural connections?

a.en: No. Machine screws are designed for equipment assembly, not structural load-bearing connections. Their shear and tension strength is significantly lower than structural bolts (Grade 8.8 or 10.9 hex bolts). For structural connections, use appropriate structural fasteners.
